claim: The dominance of word-initial glyphs o, c, q, s, d and word-final glyphs y, n, l, r, o reflects a Vigenere-style polyalphabetic cipher in which cipher-alphabet assignment is positionally determined within the word (position 1, position-final), producing artificial initial/final glyph constraints that do not reflect underlying language phonotactics.
evidence_for: Strict positional constraints on glyphs (e.g., 'q' almost always followed by 'o', certain glyphs never word-final) are more consistent with cipher rules than natural phonotactics
A Vigenere cipher keyed by word-position would produce exactly this pattern: fixed alphabets for position 1 and position-N inflate specific glyphs at those slots
Word length distribution peaking at 5.03 characters is consistent with a fixed-width encoding block
evidence_against: Vigenere ciphers typically produce entropy closer to the key-alphabet entropy; observed 3.86 bits is lower than expected for a strong polyalphabetic cipher
Natural languages also show strong positional phonotactic constraints (Latin: -us, -a, -um endings), so positional bias alone does not confirm polyalphabetic cipher
Prior belief [0.05] assigns very low probability to positional constraints reflecting cipher structure
test: For each word-position p (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, final), compute the conditional entropy H(glyph | position=p). Under a Vigenere cipher, H(glyph | position) should be uniform across positions and equal to the single-alphabet entropy. Under natural language, H(glyph | position) should decrease at terminal positions due to morphological endings. Compare the positional entropy profile to profiles from: (a) Vigenere-enciphered medieval Latin, (b) plaintext medieval Latin, (c) a random glyph sequence with matching unigram distribution.
test_metric: Per-position conditional glyph entropy H(g | pos) for positions 1-5 and final, compared to Vigenere-enciphered Latin and plaintext Latin benchmarks
test_threshold: If H(g | pos) is approximately uniform across positions (variance < 0.05 bitsÂ²) and matches Vigenere-enciphered Latin profile within 0.1 bits per position, polyalphabetic positional cipher is supported; if terminal positions show significantly lower H, natural morphological constraints are indicated

reasoning: This test directly distinguishes cipher-imposed positional constraints from language-phonotactic positional constraints, which is necessary to determine whether the glyph positional rules should be modeled as cipher structure or grammatical structure in any decipherment attempt.
